toxicity.notes: Completely safe for all fish, axolotls, and aquatic turtles. One of the most recommended aquarium plants.

specialNotes: Never bury the rhizome — attach to driftwood or rock with thread or gel superglue
Brown patches on older leaves are normal and not disease

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for all aquarium inhabitants including fish, shrimp, and snails.

specialNotes: Attach to hardscape — burying the rhizome causes rot
Slow growing but extremely hardy
Algae on leaves can be cleaned with a toothbrush

toxicity.notes: 100% safe for all fish, shrimp, snails, and amphibians. Popular spawning medium for fish.

specialNotes: Tie to mesh, driftwood, or rocks — free-floating works too
Excellent fry and shrimp habitat
Tolerates a very wide temperature range

toxicity.notes: Safe for all freshwater fish and invertebrates.

specialNotes: Needs nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs
Can grow very large — best for 50L+ tanks
Provide CO2 for optimal growth

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for all aquarium species.

specialNotes: Fast grower — great for nutrient export and algae control
Leaves change shape dramatically based on CO2 and light levels
Can be grown floating or planted

toxicity.notes: Completely safe for fish, amphibians, and invertebrates. Releases allelopathic chemicals that can slow algae.

specialNotes: Rootless — can float or be loosely anchored
Very fast growth rate, may need thinning weekly
Hardy down to near-freezing — pond safe in many climates

toxicity.notes: Safe and edible for many fish, turtles, and waterfowl. Natural food source.

specialNotes: Extremely fast growth — can cover entire surface in days
Excellent nitrate export and filtration support
Blocks light below — monitor submerged plants

toxicity.notes: Safe for pond fish and turtles. Dangling roots provide cover for fish fry.

specialNotes: Needs high light and warm temperatures to thrive
Do not let water splash onto the leaves — causes rot
Can be invasive outdoors in tropical climates

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for all fish, shrimp, and snails.

specialNotes: May melt when first introduced — normal, will recover
Prefers nutrient-rich substrate
Comes in green, brown, and red varieties

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for all freshwater fish and invertebrates.

specialNotes: High light needed for red coloration — green in low light
CO2 injection improves growth and color dramatically
Fast grower — trim regularly

toxicity.notes: Safe for all fish. African cichlids will eat it — suitable for cichlid tanks as a food source.

specialNotes: Spreads rapidly via runners — fills tank background quickly
Does not tolerate liquid CO2 supplements (Excel/Flourish) well
Best planted in small groups and allowed to multiply

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for shrimp, snails, and all fish.

specialNotes: Popular carpet plant — needs high light and CO2 to form a thick carpet
Pearls oxygen bubbles (pearling) when healthy
Can be grown in a dry start method before flooding

toxicity.notes: Safe for all aquarium inhabitants.

specialNotes: Smallest aquarium plant — creates ultra-fine carpet
Requires CO2 injection, high light, and nutrient-rich substrate
Not beginner-friendly — demanding but stunning when established

toxicity.notes: Safe for pond fish and turtles. Dangling roots make excellent fry cover and biofilm grazing surface.

specialNotes: Thrives in full sun outdoors
Extremely invasive in the wild — do not release into natural waterways
Beautiful purple flowers when grown in good conditions
Can double in size in a week under ideal conditions

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for all aquarium inhabitants.

specialNotes: Forms a grassy carpet with medium light — no CO2 required
Spreads rapidly via runners once established
Great alternative to HC for lower-tech setups

toxicity.notes: Considered reptile-vivarium safe — most herp keepers use it without issue. Contains calcium oxalate crystals which are toxic to cats, dogs, and birds. Not recommended in enclosures with mammals or birds.

specialNotes: One of the most popular vivarium plants — nearly indestructible
Thrives in very low light making it perfect for bioactive setups
Roots can trail into water for semi-hydro setups

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and reptile-safe. Commonly used in tropical vivarium setups.

specialNotes: Dramatically droops when thirsty — bounces back quickly with water
Prefers humidity above 70% — thrives in enclosed vivariums
Beautiful leaf patterning in green/red/white varieties

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic to all common reptiles, amphibians, mammals, and birds per ASPCA.

specialNotes: Stores water in leaves — somewhat drought tolerant
Hundreds of varieties available with different leaf shapes and colors
Compact size makes it ideal for smaller enclosures

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for reptiles, amphibians, and mammals.

specialNotes: Fast growing — may need regular pruning in vivariums
Pilea peperomioides (Chinese Money Plant) is a popular alternative
Can get leggy — pinch tips to encourage bushy growth

toxicity.notes: Safe for reptiles and amphibians. Mildly toxic to cats and dogs (causes vomiting and diarrhea). Safe according to ASPCA for humans.

specialNotes: Produces cascading 'spiderettes' — easy to propagate
Very tolerant of neglect and low light
Excellent for hanging or trailing in tall enclosures

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for reptiles, amphibians, and all other pets.

specialNotes: Do not pour water into the central 'nest' — causes rot
Thrives in high humidity vivariums
Great focal plant for tropical setups — bold, structural look

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for reptiles and amphibians.

specialNotes: Unlike most ferns, tolerates lower humidity than average
Compact, trailing growth — good for ledges and backgrounds
Prefers cooler temperatures — avoid warm vivariums

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and reptile-safe.

specialNotes: Furry rhizomes grow over the surface — do not bury them
Epiphytic — can be mounted on cork or driftwood
Distinct, interesting texture for vivarium backgrounds

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for reptiles and other pets.

specialNotes: Mount on cork bark or a wooden board — never pot it
Water by dunking the mount weekly (or less in humid vivariums)
The brown shield fronds are normal — do not remove them

toxicity.notes: Contact irritant — sap can cause skin/mouth irritation. Generally considered reptile-safe in vivariums as reptiles rarely eat it. Not suitable for mammal or bird enclosures.

specialNotes: Extremely fast growing — great ground cover
The purple/silver striped varieties are striking in vivariums
May need frequent trimming to prevent overgrowth

toxicity.notes: Milky sap can irritate mucous membranes. Widely used in reptile vivariums without issue. Ficus fruit is toxic to cats and dogs. Not recommended in mammal enclosures.

specialNotes: Self-adhering — will climb and cover any surface including glass
Excellent background plant — creates naturalistic wall of foliage
Very fast growing under high humidity

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and commonly used in reptile and dart frog vivariums. The central 'tank' holds water that provides drinking and bathing opportunities for small frogs.

specialNotes: Fill the central cup with fresh water regularly — frogs will use it
Parent plant dies after flowering, replaced by pups
Incredibly varied — hundreds of colors and patterns available

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for all reptiles, amphibians, and mammals.

specialNotes: No soil needed — absorb nutrients and water through their leaves
Mist 2-3x per week or soak weekly for 20 minutes
Bloom once in their life then produce pups

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Widely used as a nesting material and decor in reptile enclosures.

specialNotes: Drape from branches, cork, or hooks — no substrate needed
Loves high humidity — mist frequently
Provides excellent perching texture and cover for small reptiles

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for reptiles and amphibians. Bark-based potting media is fine for vivarium use.

specialNotes: Do not let roots sit in water — overwatering is the main killer
Excellent for tropical high-humidity vivariums
Reblooms under cooler night temperatures (~15°C drop)

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for reptiles. Unlike aloe, haworthia is considered completely safe.

specialNotes: Perfect for arid tortoise and uromastyx enclosures
Only water when soil is completely dry — drought tolerant
Can tolerate lower light than most succulents

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for reptiles and mammals per ASPCA.

specialNotes: Needs bright light to maintain compact rosette shape
Ideal for desert/arid reptile enclosures — tortoises, uromastyx, bearded dragons
Dozens of colors and textures available

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for reptiles including tortoises.

specialNotes: More shade tolerant than most succulents — good for enclosures with UVB but no direct sun
Slower growing than echeveria
Related to aloe but non-toxic

toxicity.notes: Toxic to dogs, cats, and birds — causes vomiting, diarrhea, and lethargy. Generally listed as unsafe for tortoise consumption (can cause digestive issues). Avoid in enclosures where animals may ingest it.

specialNotes: One of the most common succulents — important to note toxicity
The gel inside is the edible/medicinal part; the latex beneath the skin is toxic
Only use as pure decor in enclosures where it cannot be eaten

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for reptiles and pets.

specialNotes: Incredibly cold-hardy — survives freezing temperatures
Perfect for outdoor tortoise enclosures in temperate climates
Parent rosette dies after flowering, replaced by 'chicks'

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic and safe for all reptiles, amphibians, and invertebrates. Widely used in bioactive enclosures.

specialNotes: Best applied as a mat — press firmly onto moist substrate
Prefers cool temperatures — may struggle above 24°C
Great for dart frog, salamander, and isopod enclosures

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Widely used as a substrate component for humidity-loving reptiles and amphibians.

specialNotes: Excellent moisture retention — holds 20x its weight in water
Can be used live (green) or dried as a substrate additive
Slightly acidic pH when fresh — ideal for dart frogs and nepenthes

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Safe for all vivarium inhabitants.

specialNotes: Forms beautiful dome-shaped cushions
Turns pale/silver when stressed — mist heavily to restore green
Great for naturalistic temperate and tropical vivarium floors

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. The traps cannot harm reptiles or other animals — they are designed for small insects only.

specialNotes: Only water with distilled, RO, or rainwater — tap water kills them
Requires a winter dormancy (2-3 months at <10°C)
Do not trigger traps repeatedly — wastes plant energy

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. The sticky mucilage only traps insects — harmless to reptiles and amphibians.

specialNotes: Pure water only — no tap water
One of the easiest carnivorous plants
Hundreds of species range from tropical to temperate

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. The pitchers only trap insects — safe for all reptiles and amphibians.

specialNotes: Requires a 3-4 month cold dormancy in winter
Full sun outdoors or very bright artificial light needed
Pure water only — never fertilize soil

toxicity.notes: Non-toxic. Many species actually form mutualistic relationships with small animals (tree shrews, frogs) that use the pitchers.

specialNotes: Tropical — no dormancy needed, unlike Sarracenia
Pitchers contain digestive fluid — fascinating but harmless to reptiles
Highland species need cool nights (15-18°C) to produce good pitchers
